description: KB_case: water treatment case studies extracted from EPA CWSRF and Water Reuse program documents

cases.title: Cedar Rapids WPCF Emerging Contaminants Project
cases.outcome: The City is studying the fate of PFAS in solids after incineration and evaluating treatment options for PFAS in liquid streams.

cases.title: Conway Landfill Leachate Treatment for Emerging Contaminants (PFAS)
cases.outcome: The project aims to investigate treatment options to reduce or remove PFAS in the landfill leachate stream, conduct pilot projects at the WWTF, and prepare a project for full design and construction.

cases.title: CWSRF Emerging Contaminants Project Eligibility Decision Tool
cases.outcome: The document provides a decision tool for determining CWSRF eligibility for emerging contaminant projects, including identifying the contaminants, selecting appropriate treatment technologies, and conducting necessary planning and design activities.

cases.title: Clean Water Emerging Contaminants Funding
treatment_chain: Groundwater and surface water protection and restoration
cases.outcome: The funding provides low-cost financing and grants to communities and Tribes for a wide range of water quality infrastructure projects to address emerging contaminants in wastewater, stormwater, and nonpoint source pollution.

cases.title: CWSRF Emerging Contaminants Presentation
Using CWSRF Emerging Contaminants (EC) Funds to Tackle New or Re-emerging Contaminants
cases.outcome: The projects aim to address emerging contaminants such as PFAS, PPCPs, and other contaminants through various treatment methods including membrane filtration, ozone disinfection, and supercritical water oxidation.

cases.title: PFAS Destruction in Biosolids at City of Orlando Water Reclamation Division
cases.outcome: The City of Orlando is evaluating the use of Supercritical Water Oxidation to destroy PFAS and other emerging contaminants in biosolids, with the goal of reducing dependence on land application and addressing PFAS contamination.

cases.title: Lagoon Decommissioning and Sludge Disposal for PFAS Removal in Belding, Michigan
cases.outcome: The City of Belding will decommission its existing lagoon system and remove and properly dispose of PFAS-contaminated sludge to prevent future groundwater contamination. A new mechanical WWTP will be constructed to replace the lagoon system and meet effluent limits.

cases.title: Montana DPHHS Laboratory Equipment Purchase for Emerging Contaminants Monitoring
cases.outcome: The project will enable the Montana DPHHS to expand its monitoring capabilities for emerging contaminants in wastewater and surface water samples, and help the state identify communities with emerging contaminant issues and support planning and assessment activities for capital projects.

cases.title: New York State's Solicitation Process for Source Water Protection Planning Projects
cases.outcome: The State of New York successfully solicited 11 emerging contaminants (EC) projects for its Clean Water State Revolving Fund (CWSRF) federal fiscal Year 2022 (FFY22) funds. The projects are primarily planning efforts focused on addressing PFAS and 1,4-dioxane in source water and landfill leachate.

cases.title: Land Application of Biosolids and Yard Waste Compost Demonstration Project
cases.outcome: The study will evaluate the fate and transport of emerging contaminants after simulated land application of dewatered, digested biosolids, composted yard waste, and co-composted biosolids and yard waste to determine if co-composting can reduce potential contamination of the City's drinking water supply.

cases.title: Central Oklahoma Master Conservancy District Lake Thunderbird Emerging Contaminants Assessment Project
cases.outcome: The project aims to evaluate and potentially treat the stormwater and diffuse sources of pollution feeding the Lake Thunderbird tributaries to remove emerging contaminants.

cases.title: Characteristics and Treatment of Pharmaceuticals and Personal Care Products (PPCPs) in Wastewater
cases.outcome: Conventional municipal wastewater treatment facilities are not currently designed to specifically target PPCPs, but can partially remove many PPCPs to varying degrees. Additional physical, chemical, and biological wastewater treatment technologies can be used to further treat PPCPs.

cases.title: Southern Nevada Septic-to-Sewer Conversion for Emerging Contaminants
cases.outcome: The project aims to reduce the potential for nitrate and PPCPs to leach into the groundwater from septic systems by connecting houses to the nearby wastewater treatment plant, which is permitted to treat up to 150 MGD using biological nutrient removal, filtration, and UV disinfection. A portion of the treated effluent is further treated using membrane filtration and ozone disinfection for reuse.

cases.title: Tucson Water Reclaimed Water Emerging Contaminants Project
cases.outcome: The proposed treatment process includes ultraviolet light/hydrogen peroxide advanced oxidation (UV/AOP) process for removal of 1,4-dioxane and granular activated carbon (GAC) for treatment of PFAS.

cases.title: Stormwater treatment for 6PPD and 6PPD-quinone in Thornton Creek, Seattle
cases.outcome: The bioretention cells are designed to treat stormwater and reduce 6PPD and 6PPD-quinone loadings to Thornton Creek, as well as treat for other pollutants like total suspended solids, oil, dissolved copper, dissolved zinc and total phosphorus.

cases.title: One Water NYC: 2018 Water Demand Management Plan
cases.outcome: The plan aims to drive down water demand by an additional 10 million gallons of water per day by 2022 through partnerships and incentives for water-efficient technologies.

cases.title: D.C.'s Canal Park Captures Stormwater Reuse Benefits
cases.outcome: The stormwater capture and reuse system collects an estimated three million gallons of water per year, with an estimated one million gallons per year of captured stormwater treated and reused within the park to meet demand for irrigation, an ice rink, and two interactive water features.

cases.title: Water Reuse in the West: State Programs and Institutional Issues
cases.outcome: The report provides an overview of water reuse governance and programs in western U.S. states, including information on state legal and regulatory frameworks, reuse categories and treatment standards, and survey responses on state water reuse activities.

cases.title: Compliance Tips for Small Wastewater Treatment Lagoons with Clean Water Act Discharge Permits
cases.outcome: The document provides compliance tips for small wastewater treatment lagoons to achieve and maintain compliance with their NPDES permits.
references: U.S. EPA, 2011, Principles of Design and Operations of Wastewater Treatment Pond Systems for Plant Operators, Engineers, and Managers (EPA/600/R-11/088). Office of Research and Development, Washington, DC
Lagoon Systems in Maine, Troubleshooting Wastewater Lagoons.

Optimizing Performance of Facultative Wastewater Lagoon Systems Webinars Part 1 and 2, February and March 2020. Hosted by U.S. EPA Office of Compliance. Presentation by Steve Harris, President, H&S Environmental, LLC.
U.S. EPA, 2002, Wastewater Technology Fact Sheet: Chlorine Disinfection

cases.title: Shakopee Mdewakanton Sioux Community Water Reclamation Facility NPDES Permit
cases.outcome: The treatment facility is designed to remove oxygen-demanding wastes, particulates, phosphorus, and pathogens from the wastewater to meet effluent limitations and discharge to a wetland complex.

cases.title: SPDES BMP CSO Annual Report 2021
cases.outcome: The report describes the performance of the combined sewer overflow (CSO) best management practices (BMPs) implemented by the City of New York Department of Environmental Protection to manage CSOs and improve water quality.

cases.title: Small-Scale Natural Wastewater Treatment Systems: Principles and Regulatory Framework
cases.outcome: Natural systems transform and hold on to many common pollutants in household wastewater. They can provide added benefits compared to conventional treatment systems.

cases.title: NSF/ANSI Standard 350 for Water Reuse Treatment Systems
cases.outcome: The NSF/ANSI 350 standard sets clear, rigid, yet realistic guidelines for water reuse treatment systems, focusing on public health and appropriate water quality criteria for reuse applications.

cases.title: Native American Tribe uses Water Reuse
cases.outcome: The water reclamation facility treats municipal wastewater to be reused for landscape irrigation and environmental restoration, reducing demand on local groundwater supplies and supporting biodiversity.

cases.title: Water Reuse Case Study: The Alaska Water and Sewer Challenge
cases.outcome: The selected design consisting of an onsite greywater recycling system coupled with urine-diverting dry toilets could significantly reduce the reliance on imported water for non-potable uses and limit the need to export sewage for treatment and disposal.

cases.title: Water Reuse Case Study: Brooklyn, NY
cases.outcome: The treatment system is able to process 400,000 gallons per day (gpd) (approximately 1,500,000 liters per day) of wastewater. Half of the treated wastewater will be reused onsite and the remainder will be discharged directly to the East River after treatment.

cases.title: Water Reuse Case Study: Collier County, Florida
cases.outcome: The Living Machine® treats wastewater generated onsite from the facility restrooms and recycles it for toilet flushing, effectively reducing demand on potable, freshwater supplies and helping to conserve groundwater.

cases.title: Water Reuse Case Study: Fairfax County, Virginia
cases.outcome: Fairfax County reuses its treated municipal wastewater for centralized non-potable applications to offset potable water demands, address nutrient loading and permit compliance, and mitigate TMDL requirements.

cases.title: Water Reuse Case Study: Richmond Hill, Georgia
cases.outcome: The facility uses the reclaimed water for landscape irrigation and for maintaining wetlands on the property. Other benefits include reducing groundwater overdrafts, preventing saltwater intrusion, creating wildlife habitat, increasing wastewater treatment capacity, and educating the public.

cases.title: Stormwater-Sewer Diversion for Recycled Water in Los Angeles County, California
cases.outcome: Diversion of stormwater into sanitary sewers to increase non-potable recycled water supply and indirect potable water reuse.

cases.title: Water Reuse Case Study: Oswego, Kansas
cases.outcome: The reclaimed water provides a steady supply for golf course irrigation, ensuring continued operation during droughts.

cases.title: Water Reuse Case Study: Quincy, Washington
cases.outcome: The water recycling helps to lower the use of potable groundwater supplies in the region while supplying cooling water of a higher quality to the Microsoft data center.

cases.title: Water Reuse Case Study: Richmond Hill, Georgia
cases.outcome: The new water reclamation facility treats 3 million gallons per day of municipal wastewater and uses the reclaimed water for landscape irrigation and for maintaining wetlands on the property.
